name: Active Love
tagline: Dissolve resentment by generating love and sending it outward.
situations: Replaying what someone did to you
Stuck in resentment you can't release
Anger that's hurting you more than them
Before a hard conversation with someone you resent
summary: Active Love is Stutz's antidote to the trap of resentment, where you keep mentally attacking someone who has wronged you while only poisoning yourself. You deliberately generate a feeling of boundless love and direct it at the very person you're angry at — not to excuse them, but to free yourself from the grip of the grudge.
howItWorks: Resentment keeps you energetically tied to the person who hurt you, replaying the offense and prolonging your own suffering. Active Love interrupts the loop by replacing the outgoing flow of anger with an outgoing flow of love, which the mind cannot sustain alongside resentment. The point isn't the other person — it's reclaiming your own inner state and the energy the grudge was consuming.

steps.instruction: Picture your whole body surrounded by a warm, infinitely loving energy.
steps.note: Imagine a universe of love concentrating in your chest.

steps.instruction: Feel that love gather in your heart until it's full.
Send all of it toward the person you resent, holding nothing back.
Feel the love return to you, and notice the resentment loosen.

whenToUse: When resentment is looping and won't let go
Before a difficult conversation you're approaching with anger
When a grudge is costing you more than the other person
When anger is blocking another tool like Reversal of Desire
whenNotToUse: To bypass setting a needed boundary with someone harmful
In ongoing abuse — safety and distance come first
As pressure to 'forgive and forget' before you're ready
tips: This is for your freedom, not their absolution
Do it fully — generate real warmth, not a grudging gesture
It pairs with, it doesn't replace, real boundaries

evidence.summary: A clinical-practice tool. Its aim overlaps with loving-kindness and forgiveness interventions, which have growing research support for reducing anger and improving well-being.
citations: Stutz, P. & Michels, B. (2012). The Tools. Spiegel & Grau.
